speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number six and the survivor is Mr. Victor Dortheimer. Before the tape break, you were saying that you had returned to your house in Krakow, and that it was occupied, and your wife's house was as well. Did you ever find out about what happened to your belongings that was in the house? Or did you try to reclaim them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I didn't, because they got more right than-- the same right, like me. Because the widow was with the children. I cannot put her away. You know, [INAUDIBLE]. And so I left it. The same was with my wife flat. I went to this office, what they giving you flats. And they said to me, OK, if you show me one empty flat, I'm giving you. But nowhere empty-- empty flat-- finished. So I decide I cannot stay here anymore. I'm going to Czechoslovakia. And we went Czechoslovakia-- we went to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me--

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my wife. This was '45. And I was staying until '49, when I went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you were in Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Painting and decorating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In same factory what I was [INAUDIBLE] by Schindler, because they took over the Czech textile fabric.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of that factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: From beginning, it was [? Leffburg. ?] That was the first owner. But after this, the factory was Mosilana. It was state, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you know anybody that was working with you? Were-- was any other-- were any other Schindler--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just one. Just me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you stay in contact with any of the Schindler survivors that you were with?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was-- no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you were in Czechoslovakia, you were painting. Were you were working for yourself, or were you working for someone else?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was employed in factory, because everything was state's.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until '49, when I went, '49, to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you do when you were in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Painting. Decorating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you find it difficult adapting and changing your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I lost my homeland, what was Krakow, I-- nowhere I-- I've-- I found my homeland, until today.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you call England your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: I [? think ?] better in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you leave Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is circumstances, you know. My wife didn't like to stay in Israel. Altogether, it's too hot, you know. I didn't know then the climate, and so we left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was with Helena?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was-- this-- I-- I divorced Helena last time, '57.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she'd been with you right the way? She had been through it with Schindler as well. And had she been with you right the way through?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until liberating, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Until you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet Lydia?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '57, '58, in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. She got two children. I have one child.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your child named?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, two grandchildren. My-- I have-- mine grandchildren, two grandchildren, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where do they live?

speaker: interviewee
text: Please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where do they live? Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you say that your experiences have affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again?

speaker: interviewer
text: How have your experiences affected your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Experience?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: After my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Through-- with work-- being in the Holocaust, being involved. How has that affected you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just I didn't forget what happened to us. Never I forget it. I am everything satisfied, what I got. I have no ambition to be the rich. And I am happy that I got grandchildren. And that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the rest of your family? What happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: As you know-- I don't-- I told you, OK. My parents-- my father was killed, '45, in Mauthausen. My mother was killed in Belzec, '42. My brother was shot-- David-- in Plaszow, '43. And my younger brother fell in [INAUDIBLE] Russia-- again, German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to any other family members?

speaker: interviewee
text: The several, 90%, killed from the German. And 10% is alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: With-- referring to the film, how true is it to what actually happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean, how?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, the portrayal, how how the film is portrayed, and how the events are portrayed.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Schindler list, as you know, because I told you that-- that for liquidation, the Schindler was 300 people, what was the-- the elite. But when we went to Gross-Rosen, because I suppose he knew that is going to be end of the war, and he wanted to rescue so much as possible, they wanted to take 700 men and 1,000-- and 300 women.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Schindler-- the official Schindler list, what the film is calling, was 300 people. And the rest-- this was from high crooks in Plaszow. And the rest was his friend, like Madritsch and another friend what is-- had got connection with the Jews. This was the list. But the original list was 300 men and few hundred ma-- women. And the role that Itzhak Stein had in the film--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: Itzhak Stein. The--

speaker: interviewee
text: Stern.

speaker: interviewer
text: Stein. Stern. The role that he played in the film, how true was it to the role that he played in real life?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Stern, for me, was just one close to Schindler, as a advisor in Jewish thing, because-- future, because he was Talmudist, and et cetera. But he has no influence at all in this, what happen-- no influence. The-- he came to us October of '45-- '44. And he stay until the liberation-- the liberation. That's all. But he had no function in our place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he an accountant, like the film portrayed him to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a clerk, clerk. But he was-- the most important time, from '43 until '44, he wasn't with us. He came later.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how true would you say the film is to what actually happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: This film is made on the influence of Mr. Page. Mr. Page-- I told you, I go to his-- [INAUDIBLE] what was his name? Pfefferberg. This is a film is from his point of view. And he came also to us, '44-- just few months, which is nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why are you not featured in the film?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who?

speaker: interviewer
text: You.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me? [LAUGHS] They avoid everybody to take his place. He wanted to be more important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you ever met Spielberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Spielberg? Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: The premiere. I met the Pfefferberg too in premiere, yeah. In Tel Aviv I met him. I said, you are a blo-- [LAUGHS] a bloody bluffer. But he said, eh, OK. Well, he made little bit money. But saying to him, the Schindler list exists. He was the initiator of the film, with Keneally together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you would like to say about your life, or about what happened in later years, that you haven't said already?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I-- unfortunately, I was thinking in my life maybe too many times, about my past. Even sometimes, it was-- I thought I would be happy, I was happy, I start to think about family, and why it happened. I prefer to be in Poland, to eat bread, than here eat cakes. You know? Because I lost family. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is there a message that you'd like to leave for your grandchildren or for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: No message because the youth today is another generation. I spoke to my children. They're nothing in common. They are born free. In Israel, are born free. We have been born in ghettos. And this is two mentality. They know they got homeland. We never had homeland. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Example-- in Tel Aviv, 11 o'clock, 14 years old, in the night, I said Offir, where are you going? I'm going dancing. When you coming? Oh, 4, 5 o'clock. I said, you are j-- gra-- grandfather, what [INAUDIBLE]? This is [INAUDIBLE] for a free country. This a difference. They're free people. We're not. We've never been free. We had anti-Semite, with the this-- and the same you've got here. You are not free. If you think you are free, still you are not free. Correct? Mr. Dortheimer, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe what this document is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is document, what is my mother application to get permission to stay in ghetto Krakow. And this application was made in [? 940, ?] in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was the application made to?

speaker: interviewee
text: To the German authority, to get permission to stay in the ghetto Krakow. This is my mother, orig-- origin picture what he made before they made-- he made application to stay in ghetto, [? 940. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was made in [? 947 ?] in Czechoslovakia, in Brunnlitz. This is my wife and my son what he was born in Czechoslovakia, '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helena.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my first wife, Helena. This pic-- picture was made two months after liberation, in '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is picture of my son, what is two and a half years old, in Czechoslovakia, '48.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your son's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michael. In '46--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, what I was voluntary in Czech army, in '46, in six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is picture of my brother, David who was born 190-- 1916. He was shot in Plaszow in the year 19-- 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is picture my and my wife, after two-- after two months after my wedding with her, in 1958--

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: --in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's my wife, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lydia.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's Helena, isn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm missing--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're missing Helena.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's his wife, now-- ex-wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's right. That's it. That's the main person. That's who we really want, Steve.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is wedding of my son with his bride, yeah, [? Birgit, ?] and my first wife, Helena.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were they married?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was married in Haifa, 900-- 1976.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, what was made 1945, two months after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is picture of Mr. Schindler, before he left, after the war, our camp. I going to him to said goodbye. And he gave me for memory his picture. He--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is international-- international German driving license, what I they-- what I get 1965, when I was living in Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are the people in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is picture made in Tel Aviv, with my two grandchildren, what was made 1900, approximately, 80.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your grandchildren's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Michael-- sorry, sorry, Jonathan and Offir.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one's Offir?

speaker: interviewee
text: The smaller one is Offir.

speaker: interviewer
text: The one on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest one.

speaker: interviewer
text: On which side of the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right side. The blond one. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in-- in Tel Aviv, in park in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson and my son. This is the first son, Jonathan, when he was two years old, what is must be '78.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also in Tel Aviv.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is invitation card, in Hebrew, for my-- my son, for his bar mitzvah, when he was 13. It was 1940-- '59.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- this was the-- this was celebrating in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son's bar mitzvah. It was '59, in Haifa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is not clear. Is it clear?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this is a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is myself and my wife, in Poland, [? Crenetz, ?] what is spa, in 1964.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is picture mine, my wife, and a daughter of our friend, in Jerusalem, 1994.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's the little girl's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Alanna. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is made in premiere for the-- the film, where I am together with Steven Spielberg and his guards, '94.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what premiere in this, what is a kino?

speaker: interviewer
text: It's in-- what-- well, what city is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Kino Empire, in Leicester Square.

speaker: interviewer
text: In London, England.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is made in '94 on the grave of Schindler what I am coming to give him the honor, to bring a stone from Auschwitz to put on his grave.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was his grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is made in Krakow, Plaszow in ex-concentration camp. And this picture is a picture of villa of the killer, Amon Goth, what he was living here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was made after the war, '94, in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is made '94. And this picture is the producing manager of Carlton Television, [INAUDIBLE], the director of factory. And this factory was the concentration camp in the war-- Schindler factory-- [INAUDIBLE] there and myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe this picture? This picture is made in '94. This is factory what was Schindler factory until [? 944. ?] Today is state factory. Thank you. Could you please describe this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture-- this picture was made in Auschwitz, '94. And it is at concentration camp Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who are the people with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: These people-- this was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] not a poli-- the cameraman, cameraman. That's a cameraman.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. What's this a picture of?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is ex-factory of Schindler. Today it's in another factory in Czechoslovakia. This picture was made in '94.
